level 2  It took me a minute to realize that the hologram was live! (It’s OP standing just off to the side if anyone else missed that)
  level 2  This is better than a lot of the “professional” hologram displays I’ve seen.
 Don’t know why I air-quoted that word but I’ve accepted it and so shall we all.
      level 2  Oh I know that feeling of being overwhelmed by this project sooooo well. I spend months trying not to despair and only look at the one specific problem currently ahead. Here is an 'as short as I can make it'- writeup :)
 It just started with this image in my head:  https://vimeo.com/101996957 and the thought that most PoV-Displays were kiind of uncool because they are often either just 2D or can only play some canned animations.
 I spend the first 6 weeks (I kid you not) just trying to get a stable, still image onto the display with my ESP32. Most libraries are made for 30fps color, I needed 2000fps monochrome, so I couldn't use those.
 After 6 weeks I had one solid color without flickering. Then I spend a lot of time to get TCP/IP data transmission working between the ESP32 on the Display and my computer. I tried to send 30fps for another week. So much trial and error. Of-by-one-errors, logical problems, electrical finicyness.
 After that worked I tried to send every one of those 30 frames 30 times (my minimum target fps was 900) within one second. If the animation still looked smooth to me, it shouldve displayed 900 frames. (I didn't have a camera that fast to reliably check).
 For the content-calculation I learned the basics of compute-shaders (which sounds waaaay more scary than it is. Its basically just a for-loop on steroids). The algorithm was about 3 days of coffee-and-whiteboard-and-coffee-and-whiteboard time and then it made click and took about 10 lines of code in the end :)
 So yeah: just methodical work from the ground up, just starting to solve some problems, then some more, then run away from some (try to do it elegantly!), solve some more and eventually... yeah, your deadline will arrive before there are no more problems. But you get the point!
    level 2  Looks like a kinect is used to render a point cloud into a cylindrical POV display
        level 2  Well, not really. This is just a Kinect Depth Camera live-scanning me. So whatever movements I make, it shows. If I move "out" of the area the display covers, I disappear :)
  level 1  Now - Imagine you replace that - with an full-color, high-res OLED panel, and rotate it 360. 30 rotations per second - and you'd have full motion Holographics.

这家成立三年的总部位于古尔冈的初创公司表示，SIG和詹姆斯·默多克的Lupa Systems领投了3,100万美元的B轮融资。现有投资者Sequoia Capital India，Omidyar Network India和Waterbridge Ventures也参与了这一轮融资，迄今为止，该初创公司的融资总额已达到约5000万美元。
 Doubtnut应用程序允许学生为问题拍照，并使用机器学习和图像识别功能通过短视频提供答案。这些视频为学生提供了解决问题的分步说明。
  该公司表示，该应用程序支持多种语言，并且每天吸引超过250万活跃用户，每月在该应用程序上花费6亿分钟。这家初创企业表示，超过一半的用户在过去12个月中首次上线。
 这家初创公司表示，它已经开发出了超过6500万个问题的库，其中有9种语言可供6年级至高中生使用。与其他几家受欢迎的edtech公司不同，Doudbnut说，其应用程序可吸引较小城镇的学生。该公司表示：“目前有85％的用户来自印度排名前15位的城市之外，还有60％的用户在州议会学习，而这些州的典型教学语言是当地的本地语言。”
 TechCrunch去年报道说，Byju正在进行谈判，以高达1.5亿美元的价格收购Doubtnut。后来Byju降低了报价，此后两家公司结束了谈判。
 “建立Doubtnut的目标是改善所有学生的学习成果，尤其是印度主要城市以外的学生。我们专注于以本地语言开发内容，并使用技术为这一庞大目标群体的人们提供价格合理的解决方案。” Doubtnut联合创始人兼首席执行官Tanushree Nagori说道，并补充说，
 “我们很高兴欢迎SIG和Lupa的加入； SIG拥有在全球范围内投资高科技企业的丰富经验，而Lupa Systems则拥有建立世界一流企业和利用高影响力技术的无与伦比的经验，”她补充说。 
这家初创公司表示将动用新资金来增加对更多语言的支持，并扩大其今天涵盖的主题范围。 Doubtnut还计划引入付费课程。
note_en:
The three-year-old Gurgaon-headquartered startup said SIG and James Murdoch’s Lupa Systems led the $31 million Series B funding round. Existing investors Sequoia Capital India, Omidyar Network India and Waterbridge Ventures also participated in the round, which brings the startup’s to-date raise to about $50 million to date.
 The Doubtnut app allows students to take a picture of a problem, and uses machine learning and image recognition to deliver their answers through short-videos. These videos offer students step-by-step instructions to solve a problem.
  The app supports multiple languages, and has amassed over 2.5 million daily active users who spend 600 million minutes a month on the app, the startup said. More than half of the users have come online for the first time in last 12 months, the startup said.
 The startup said it has developed a bank of over 65 million questions in nine languages for students from sixth grade to high-school. Unlike several other popular edtech firms, Doubtnut said its app reaches students in smaller towns and cities. “85% of the current base comes from outside of the top 15 Indian cities, and 60% users study in state boards where typical medium of instruction is the local vernacular language,” the startup said.
 TechCrunch reported last year that Byju’s was in  talks to acquire Doubtnut for as much as $150 million. Byju’s later lowered its deal offer, after which the two firms ended their talks.
 “Doubtnut has been built with a vision to improve learning outcomes for all students, especially those outside the major Indian cities. We specialize in developing content in vernacular languages and use technology to create affordable solutions for people in this large target segment,” said Tanushree Nagori, co-founder and CEO of Doubtnut, adding,
 “We are pleased to welcome onboard SIG and Lupa; SIG brings in strong experience of investing in ed-tech companies globally and Lupa Systems brings unparalleled experience of building world-class businesses and harnessing high-impact technologies,” she added.
 The startup said it will deploy the fresh capital to add support for more language and broaden the scope of subjects it covers today. Doubtnut is also planning to introduce paid courses.
